title: Triangulation Measurement Technique
description: Apply triangulation to record evidence position on a scaled crime-scene sketch. Drag an evidence item around an overhead room floor plan and read the live straight-line distances from two fixed reference corners (A and B), computed with the Pythagorean theorem. Adjust the room dimensions with sliders, and record each measurement to a log the way an investigator fills in a sketch sheet.
creator: Forensic Science Intelligent Textbook
subject: Forensic Science
Crime Scene Investigation
Evidence Collection
Crime Scene Sketching
Measurement
bloomLevel: Apply (L3) — calculate triangulation distances to record an evidence item's position on a scaled sketch.
chapter_title: Crime Scene Investigation and Evidence Collection

educational.topic: Recording the position of evidence with the triangulation method on a scaled crime-scene sketch
learningObjectives: Apply the triangulation method: measure an evidence item's straight-line distance from two fixed reference points.
Use the Pythagorean theorem to relate an item's (x, y) coordinates to its distances from two corners.
Read distances in centimeters from a scaled overhead floor plan.
Record multiple evidence items to a measurement log as on a real sketch sheet.
educational.prerequisites: Familiarity with the Pythagorean theorem and the idea of measuring from fixed reference points

pedagogical.interactionPattern: An overhead floor plan of a room fills the left ~72% of the canvas, with a 1-meter grid and two fixed green reference corners (A at upper-left, B at upper-right). A draggable orange evidence dot is connected to A and B by blue dashed lines whose midpoints show the live distance in centimeters; the dot also displays its (x, y) coordinates measured from A. The right panel shows the live distances and coordinates plus a measurement log. Two sliders set the room width and length (3-10 m). 'Record Measurement' appends the current item number and its A/B distances to the log; 'Clear Log' empties it.
pedagogical.assessment: Application with immediate feedback: the student drags the item and the distances recompute via the Pythagorean theorem, confirming whether they understand what triangulation measures. Recording several items builds a log that mirrors how positions are documented on a scaled sketch, and changing the room size tests whether the student understands that distances are measured to fixed corners.
pedagogical.modelType: Geometric model: the evidence item has a position in meters from corner A; distance to A is sqrt(x^2 + y^2) and distance to B is sqrt((W - x)^2 + y^2), where W is the room width. Pixel geometry is scaled to keep the room's real aspect ratio as the sliders change its dimensions.
controls: Draggable evidence item (orange dot) anywhere within the room
'Record Measurement' button — append the current distances to the log
'Clear Log' button — empty the measurement log
